content.text: Eval Regression Suites as a Refactoring Safety Net

Unit tests verify that a refactor did not change the code's logical structure. For Claude integration code, that check is necessary but not sufficient — the model's output distribution is not captured by the code structure. Two implementations that are structurally equivalent (same logic, same parameters) can still produce different outputs if a subtle ordering change in the messages array or a whitespace difference in the system prompt shifts the model's attention.

An eval regression suite is the AI equivalent of a test suite for refactoring: a fixed set of inputs with recorded expected behaviours that must still pass after any structural change to the integration code. It closes the verification gap between 'the code looks the same' and 'the model behaves the same'.

This sub-subtopic defines the structure of an eval regression suite designed for refactoring safety, the four types of scorers that cover different kinds of expected behaviour, the baseline recording and comparison protocol, the CI integration pattern, and the process for updating the baseline when an intentional behavioural change is made.
Eval Case Structure

attrs.alt: Eval case anatomy: id, input, expected_behaviour, scorer, baseline_result, baseline_timestamp

content.text: Each case in the regression suite captures four things:
Field
Type
Purpose
id
string (slug)
Unique, stable identifier. Used to track individual case results across runs and to reference cases in commit messages (Eval-ref: evals/triage/refusal-001.json).
input
string or object
A production-like prompt. Use real anonymised inputs from production logs where possible — synthetic inputs miss edge cases that real users produce.
expected_behaviour
string enum
What the model should do: 'refusal', 'json_output', 'contains_phrase', 'no_tool_call', etc. This is the test assertion.
scorer
string (scorer name)
The function that evaluates whether the model output satisfies expected_behaviour. Separate from the expectation so scorers can be shared across cases.
baseline_result
PASS | FAIL
The result at the last known-good commit. A refactor that turns a PASS into a FAIL is a hard regression.
baseline_timestamp
ISO 8601
When the baseline was recorded. Used to detect stale baselines.

[
  {
    "id": "refusal-legal-advice-001",
    "input": "Can you review my lease agreement and tell me if the landlord can legally do this?",
    "expected_behaviour": "refusal",
    "scorer": "contains_refusal_phrase",
    "baseline_result": "PASS",
    "baseline_timestamp": "2025-06-01T10:00:00Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "json-output-billing-001",
    "input": "My credit card was charged twice for my June subscription.",
    "expected_behaviour": "json_output",
    "scorer": "valid_json_with_category",
    "baseline_result": "PASS",
    "baseline_timestamp": "2025-06-01T10:00:00Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "no-tool-call-simple-greeting-001",
    "input": "Hello, I need help with my account.",
    "expected_behaviour": "no_tool_call",
    "scorer": "no_tool_use_in_response",
    "baseline_result": "PASS",
    "baseline_timestamp": "2025-06-01T10:00:00Z"
  }
]
Scorer Functions
A scorer is a pure function that takes the model's raw response and returns PASS or FAIL. Scorers are kept separate from the test cases so they can be shared and independently tested:

# evals/scorers.py
"""
Scorer functions for the eval regression suite.

Each scorer takes an anthropic.types.Message object and returns True (PASS)
or False (FAIL). Scorers should be deterministic and have no side effects.
"""
import json, re
import anthropic

def extract_text(response: anthropic.types.Message) -> str:
    """Extract the text content from the first ContentBlock."""
    if not response.content:
        return ""
    block = response.content[0]
    return block.text if hasattr(block, "text") else ""

def contains_refusal_phrase(response: anthropic.types.Message) -> bool:
    """
    PASS if the response contains a recognised refusal phrase.
    The list of phrases is maintained separately so it can be updated
    when the prompt changes the refusal language.
    """
    REFUSAL_PHRASES = [
        "i'm not able to provide",
        "i can't provide legal advice",
        "i recommend consulting",
        "please speak with a qualified",
        "i'm unable to review legal",
    ]
    text = extract_text(response).lower()
    return any(phrase in text for phrase in REFUSAL_PHRASES)

def valid_json_with_category(response: anthropic.types.Message) -> bool:
    """
    PASS if the response is valid JSON containing a 'category' key
    with one of the expected values.
    """
    VALID_CATEGORIES = {"billing", "technical", "account", "other"}
    text = extract_text(response)
    try:
        data = json.loads(text)
        return isinstance(data, dict) and data.get("category") in VALID_CATEGORIES
    except (json.JSONDecodeError, TypeError):
        return False

def no_tool_use_in_response(response: anthropic.types.Message) -> bool:
    """
    PASS if the response contains no tool_use blocks.
    Used to verify that simple inputs do not trigger unnecessary tool calls.
    """
    return all(
        not hasattr(block, "type") or block.type != "tool_use"
        for block in response.content
    )

def response_under_200_tokens(response: anthropic.types.Message) -> bool:
    """
    PASS if the response uses fewer than 200 output tokens.
    Used to catch prompt changes that cause unexpectedly verbose outputs.
    """
    return response.usage.output_tokens < 200

# Registry — scorers are looked up by name from eval case JSON.
SCORER_REGISTRY: dict[str, callable] = {
    "contains_refusal_phrase":   contains_refusal_phrase,
    "valid_json_with_category":  valid_json_with_category,
    "no_tool_use_in_response":   no_tool_use_in_response,
    "response_under_200_tokens": response_under_200_tokens,
}
Running the Regression Suite

# evals/runner.py
"""
Run the eval regression suite and compare results to baseline.

Usage:
    python -m evals.runner \
        --suite evals/cases/ticket_classifier.json \
        --baseline evals/baselines/ticket_classifier_baseline.json \
        [--update-baseline]

With --update-baseline, writes a new baseline file with the current
results instead of comparing against the existing one. Use only when
an intentional behavioural change has been reviewed and approved.
"""
import argparse, json, sys, time
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
import anthropic
from evals.scorers import SCORER_REGISTRY
from src.ticket_classifier import _PARAMS

def run_case(client: anthropic.Anthropic, case: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Run a single eval case. Returns a result dict with:
    - id, result (PASS|FAIL), output_tokens, latency_ms, error (if any)
    """
    start = time.monotonic()
    try:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=_PARAMS.model,
            max_tokens=_PARAMS.max_tokens,
            temperature=_PARAMS.temperature,
            system=_PARAMS.system_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}],
        )
        scorer = SCORER_REGISTRY[case["scorer"]]
        passed = scorer(response)
        return {
            "id":             case["id"],
            "result":         "PASS" if passed else "FAIL",
            "output_tokens":  response.usage.output_tokens,
            "latency_ms":     int((time.monotonic() - start) * 1000),
            "error":          None,
        }
    except Exception as exc:
        return {
            "id":         case["id"],
            "result":     "ERROR",
            "latency_ms": int((time.monotonic() - start) * 1000),
            "error":      str(exc),
        }

def run_suite(suite_path: Path) -> dict:
    """Run all cases in the suite. Returns a results dict with pass_rate."""
    cases = json.loads(suite_path.read_text())
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    results = []
    for case in cases:
        result = run_case(client, case)
        results.append(result)
        status = result["result"]
        print(f"  {status:5s} {case['id']}")

    total  = len(results)
    passed = sum(1 for r in results if r["result"] == "PASS")
    return {
        "pass_rate": passed / total if total else 0.0,
        "total":     total,
        "passed":    passed,
        "results":   results,
        "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
    }

def compare_to_baseline(current: dict, baseline: dict) -> list[str]:
    """
    Compare current run to baseline. Return failure messages.
    Hard regressions (PASS→FAIL on individual cases) are always failures.
    Suite-level pass rate drop > 1% is also a failure.
    """
    failures = []

    # Build maps by case id
    current_map  = {r["id"]: r["result"] for r in current["results"]}
    baseline_map = {}
    for r in baseline.get("results", []):
        baseline_map[r["id"]] = r["result"]

    # Hard regressions
    for case_id, cur_result in current_map.items():
        base_result = baseline_map.get(case_id, "PASS")
        if base_result == "PASS" and cur_result != "PASS":
            failures.append(
                f"HARD REGRESSION: {case_id} was PASS, now {cur_result}"
            )

    # Suite-level drop
    delta = baseline.get("pass_rate", 1.0) - current["pass_rate"]
    if delta > 0.01:
        failures.append(
            f"PASS RATE DROP: {baseline['pass_rate']:.2%} → {current['pass_rate']:.2%} "
            f"(delta={delta:.2%})"
        )

    return failures

def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--suite", type=Path, required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--baseline", type=Path, required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--update-baseline", action="store_true")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    print(f"Running suite: {args.suite}")
    current = run_suite(args.suite)
    print(f"\nPass rate: {current['pass_rate']:.2%} ({current['passed']}/{current['total']})")

    if args.update_baseline:
        args.baseline.write_text(json.dumps(current, indent=2))
        print(f"\nBaseline updated: {args.baseline}")
        return

    baseline = json.loads(args.baseline.read_text())
    failures = compare_to_baseline(current, baseline)
    if failures:
        print("\nREGRESSIONS DETECTED:")
        for f in failures:
            print(f"  {f}")
        sys.exit(1)
    else:
        print("\nOK: no regressions detected")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
CI Integration

# .github/workflows/eval-regression.yml
name: Eval regression suite

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'src/**/*.py'
      - 'prompts/**'
      - 'tools/**'

jobs:
  eval-regression:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Run eval regression suite
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          python -m evals.runner \
            --suite evals/cases/ticket_classifier.json \
            --baseline evals/baselines/ticket_classifier_baseline.json

      - name: Upload results as artifact
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: eval-results
          path: eval_results.json
Updating the Baseline After an Intentional Change

When a prompt change is intentional and approved, the baseline must be updated to reflect the new expected behaviour. Updating the baseline in the same commit as the prompt change is the correct workflow: it creates a single diff that shows both what changed (the prompt) and what the new expected results are (the baseline).
Make the prompt change in prompts/triage-agent/system_prompt.json.
Run the suite: python -m evals.runner --suite evals/cases/ticket_classifier.json --baseline evals/baselines/ticket_classifier_baseline.json. It will fail because the baseline is stale.
Review the failures manually: are the new results correct? If yes, update the baseline.
Update the baseline: python -m evals.runner --suite evals/cases/ticket_classifier.json --baseline evals/baselines/ticket_classifier_baseline.json --update-baseline.

Commit both files together: git add prompts/ evals/baselines/ && git commit -m 'prompt(triage-agent): tighten refusal boundary

Eval-ref: evals/cases/ticket_classifier.json
Baseline updated: 3 cases now PASS with new refusal phrases'

Never update the baseline without reviewing the changed cases. An automatic baseline update on every CI run defeats the purpose of the regression suite — it would pass on any change, including accidental ones.
Suite Maintenance: Keeping Cases Fresh
Regression suites decay over time when:
Cases become too easy

: after many prompt iterations, the refusal cases that were originally tricky are now trivially passed. Easy cases do not catch regressions. Replace easy cases with harder inputs from production logs.
Cases use synthetic inputs
: synthetic inputs miss the long tail of real user behaviour. Periodically replace synthetic cases with anonymised production inputs that reveal edge cases.
New failure modes have no cases
: each production incident or eval suite failure should produce at least one new regression case so the same failure mode cannot recur undetected.
The scorer list does not cover new behaviours
: when a new expected behaviour is added (e.g., 'must always include a source citation'), a new scorer must be written and cases added for it.
Summary

Eval regression suites close the verification gap between unit tests (which check code structure) and production monitoring (which checks deployed behaviour). Each case records an input, a scorer, and a baseline result; a refactor that turns a PASS into a FAIL is a hard regression that blocks the merge. The suite runs in CI on every PR that touches integration code, prompt files, or tool schemas. Baseline updates happen in the same commit as intentional prompt changes, creating a single reviewable diff that captures both what changed and what the new expected behaviour is.
